questions.question: Draw a neat labelled diagram showing the flow of energy in an ecosystem. Explain why the pyramid of energy is always upright and never inverted.
questions.answer: Diagram: Sun at top → arrow to producers (grass/plants) → herbivores (rabbit/deer) → primary carnivores (frog/fox) → top carnivores (lion/hawk), with decomposers at base receiving dead matter. Labels: trophic levels, 10% transfer arrows. Pyramid of energy is always upright because only ~10% energy is transferred to next level; maximum energy at producers, progressively less at higher levels. It can never be inverted as energy cannot increase up the chain.

questions.question: If 15,000 kJ of solar energy is available to producers, calculate the energy available at the level of tertiary consumers assuming 10% law. Represent this in the form of an energy pyramid and explain the significance of the decreasing energy levels.
questions.answer: Producers: 15,000 kJ → Herbivores (primary consumers): 1,500 kJ (10%) → Secondary consumers: 150 kJ → Tertiary consumers: 15 kJ. Pyramid: wide base (producers) narrowing to top (tertiary). Significance: Limits number of trophic levels to 4–5; top carnivores get least energy, hence fewer individuals; explains why food chains are short and ecosystems maintain balance.

questions.question: Explain the process of biological magnification with the help of a labelled diagram. Discuss its long-term impact on human health and biodiversity.
questions.answer: Diagram: Water → Phytoplankton (low DDT) → Zooplankton → Small fish → Large fish → Fish-eating birds/Humans (very high DDT), arrows showing increasing concentration. Process: Non-biodegradable toxins accumulate and magnify up trophic levels. Impact: Humans – cancer, neurological disorders, reproductive issues; Biodiversity – top predators (eagles, humans) suffer population decline, reproductive failure, leading to imbalance.

questions.question: Describe how excessive use of pesticides leads to the disruption of food chains. Support your answer with a real-life example and suggest sustainable alternatives.
questions.answer: Pesticides → enter food chain → biomagnification → high concentration in top carnivores → death/reproductive failure → population crash of predators → prey (pests) increase → more pesticide use → vicious cycle. Example: DDT caused eggshell thinning in birds of prey (peregrine falcon, bald eagle) → near extinction in some areas. Alternatives: Integrated Pest Management (IPM) – biological control (ladybugs for aphids), crop rotation, neem-based biopesticides, pheromone traps.

questions.question: Explain the formation and importance of the ozone layer. Discuss in detail the mechanism by which CFCs cause its depletion and the international efforts taken to reverse the damage.
questions.answer: Ozone (O₃) forms in stratosphere when UV splits O₂ → O + O₂ → O₃; absorbs 99% harmful UV-B/C. CFC mechanism: UV breaks C–Cl bond → Cl• free radical → Cl• + O₃ → ClO + O₂ → ClO + O → Cl• + O₂ (catalytic cycle, one Cl destroys thousands of O₃). International effort: Montreal Protocol (1987) – phased out CFCs, halons; amendments strengthened it; ozone hole showing signs of recovery (projected full recovery by ~2066).
answer_outline: Catalytic depletion mechanism by Cl radicals

questions.question: Compare and contrast a natural ecosystem (forest) with an artificial ecosystem (crop field). Which one is more stable and why?
questions.answer: Natural (forest): High biodiversity, many trophic levels, self-sustaining nutrient cycling, decomposers active, no human input needed, high stability due to complexity. Artificial (crop field): Low biodiversity, mainly producers + few pests, monoculture, needs human input (fertilizers, pesticides, irrigation), low stability – easily disrupted by drought/pests/disease. Natural ecosystem more stable due to greater species diversity and self-regulation.

questions.question: Explain why energy flow is unidirectional while nutrient flow is cyclic in an ecosystem. Illustrate with a suitable example and diagram.
questions.answer: Energy: unidirectional – Sun → producers → consumers → lost as heat (second law of thermodynamics), cannot return. Nutrients: cyclic – taken by plants → passed to consumers → returned by decomposers to soil. Example: Carbon cycle – CO₂ → photosynthesis → food → respiration/decomposition → CO₂ again. Diagram: Energy (straight arrows Sun → levels → heat loss); Nutrients (circular arrows through decomposers).

questions.question: How do human activities contribute to the imbalance in the environment? Discuss any three major problems (other than ozone depletion) caused by them with their corrective measures.
questions.answer: Problems: (i) Eutrophication – excess fertilizers → algal bloom → oxygen depletion → fish kill; measure: controlled fertilizer use, buffer zones. (ii) Deforestation – loss of biodiversity, soil erosion, CO₂ increase; measure: afforestation, sustainable logging. (iii) Plastic pollution – microplastics in food chain, animal death; measure: ban single-use plastics, promote biodegradable alternatives, improve waste segregation.

questions.question: If the concentration of a persistent pesticide increases from 0.001 ppm in water to 25 ppm in fish-eating birds, calculate the magnification factor and explain the ecological consequences if this trend continues.
questions.answer: Magnification factor = 25 / 0.001 = 25,000 times. Consequences: High concentration causes eggshell thinning, reproductive failure, population decline of top predators (birds), loss of natural pest control, pest resurgence, increased pesticide use, further magnification, possible local extinction of sensitive species, food web collapse.

questions.question: Discuss the role of decomposers in nutrient cycling. What would happen to an ecosystem if decomposers were completely absent? Give reasons.
questions.answer: Role: Break complex organic matter → simple inorganic nutrients (N, P, S) → returned to soil → reused by producers. Absence: Dead organisms & waste accumulate → nutrients locked up → producers starve → food chain collapse → no energy flow → ecosystem dies. Soil fertility lost permanently, massive pollution from undecomposed matter.
